item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great format screw. (I'm not confident that's the right term, but I think "the in-universe author of the SCP is clearly affected by the SCP" counts as a format screw.) I'll upvote in a moment. One nitpick:

The subject's immune system becomes inactive. This is ultimately the most physically destructive aspect of a Sigma State, as it enables organisms from the environment (such as worms, flies, and certain microbes) to consume the subject's body over an extended period of time.

I don't think our immune systems are what prevent worms and flies from eating us, and I doubt the SCP's effect would extend to not knowing that. This sentence might be stronger if the parenthetical only mentioned microbes or was omitted entirely.

I also think the containment procedures should specify the type of gloves, since standard medical examination gloves wouldn't help. It's a "if you feel this needs to be said at all, then it should probably be said more precisely" thing. You could even make it funny in retrospect by dryly mentioning that the SCP is able to affect subjects through standard nitrile or latex medical examination gloves, so gloves made of leather or similar material are recommended.

item.title: Re: My entry for Day 1 of the 72 Hour Jam Contest.
item.content:encoded: 4514 is series 5, this is series 4, this article came first, so you shouldn't call it unoriginal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly makes observers unable to realize that people who have been stabbed are dead. They may even have lost the concept of "death".

The people who were stabbed die because they were stabbed. There's nothing anomalous about that part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, does this object makes a person undead?

UPD: Sorry, missed that the person becoming inactive. So it's not undeath, it's an actual death. What's so anomalous about that? The only possible explanation is that in the universe with SCP-3154, immortality is the norm and death is an anomaly. But we already have such an object. Since death is never mentioned in the article, it's possible that a lot of time passed and researches just no more know what a death is.

Another explanation is that it makes dead people appear alive. But we already have such an object.

item.title: Re: My entry for Day 1 of the 72 Hour Jam Contest.
item.content:encoded: We have mysterious deaths. We have dead people appearing alive. We have killing despite ΩK. What else does this object offer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe duck typing isn't that bad of a concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually an infohazard bound to the shard, and anyone it kills, which makes an observer of the event, the subject following the event, or even documentation describing it forget that death is A Thing and that the set of properties exhibited by "affected" subjects are nothing special in terms of What Happens When You Stab Someone With A 7-by-3-inch Shard Of Glass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be the only time I ever upvote a generic thing that kills you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Again with the SCP-Js being posted to the mainlist! At first, I thought I was the problem. Maybe I just didn't get it.

The actual effect here is that it makes you unable to comprehend deaths that are caused by it, which isn't much of a joke.

I thought lolfoundation was off-limits!

Very little, except plagerism and stuff that violates Wikidot's terms of service is actually off-limits. Articles succeed if no more than nine more people dislike it than like it. That's all.

What's probably going to earn that deletion, and what people really mean when they complain about "LOLFoundation" are completely irresponsible, immature, and out-of-character actions or utterly implausible events inserted as one-liners that are neither supported by, nor support, the narrative, typically using one of the old author avatar characters. Like tests with Dr. King that suddenly and inexplicably transform it into an apple-related anomaly, or O5 notes that specify that Dr. Bright isn't allowed to use the object as an extremely ill-advised substitute in some everyday task.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You forgot the evacuation of the bowels, though that might give it away immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again with the SCP-Js being posted to the mainlist! At first, I thought I was the problem. Maybe I just didn't get it. Then, I read the comments section. How is this happening in 2018? I thought lolfoundation was off-limits!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…I am assuming this is from an alternate universe where no one ever dies, and the shard of glass (would be in our universe) non anomalous . Am I the only one thinking that?

I guess alternatively the anomaly is that the glass makes you not realize that death is a thing relating to its victims?

Or is there no mystery and the Foundation is on clueless pills today?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An Ethics Committee exists? And this is where they choose to draw the line?

Love this. Had to re-read it a couple of times to make sure I hadn't missed anything & it was just the Foundation being dumbasses their usual selves affected by the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Infohazards remain my favourite scip type, and this is one of the best ones I've seen. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, okay.

This is fucking clever. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, in the most literal and straightforward sense imaginable, a thing what kills you. And you've made it into a clever article.

You absolute madman. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the best part of this anomaly is the fridge logic that comes with realizing the Ethics Committee are having meetings where they debate the ethical treatment of corpses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …dammit, how did I miss that? That's cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing. I'm sitting there going, wait, this is just a glass shard that kills people when you kill them with it, and then I realized they don't realize that. Fun!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone considered cross testing with 447?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dramatic irony is closer to what I was going for, anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the interpretation here of the contest theme. I knew where it was going by the first sentence of the second paragraph, so there was less piecing together for me than I would've liked, but there's still enough delicious, delicious dramatic irony to satisfy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, I get it. It's a murder mystery because the murder is mysterious…but, if they're still alive despite having most of their bodily functions shut off, were they murdered?

Can't tell if it's really clever or just pretending to be, but either way I'm a sucker for subversion, and I like how you turned the contest prompt on its head, so +1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! I started grinning through that tongue-in-cheek description of rigor, and laughed outright at the 'D-Class in Sigma state are unsuited for testing' bit. This is morbidly hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ….this is just like that water skip isn't it? god damn it is clever. -1 => +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderfully bonkers. It's the type of fun gag skip that I get excited to tell my friends about. "You won't believe what I just read on the wiki today…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me a moment to get it, but when I did it I physically leant back and rubbed my face. Goddammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the interpretation of murder mystery here. Really clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But they're clearly dead. Unless it's a murder weapon that censors out the idea of being murdered!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat, I like it.

item.title: My entry for Day 1 of the 72 Hour Jam Contest.
item.content:encoded:
Murder ITSELF is the mystery.

Thanks to nobody, because this is a coldpost boldpost. I cribbed the symptoms of death from Wikipedia's article on death, which doesn't have it cited. Shit's Creative Commons anyways so I'm not too worried.

SPOILERS
– hide block

The element that's a given in your standard murder mystery is that someone has, at a minimum, died. So what happens if you make that element mysterious?
